text: Ultra-marathon runners should consume a moderate-to-high carbohydrate diet of approximately 60% of energy intake (5-8 g/kg/day) to mitigate chronic training-induced glycogen depletion.
Limiting carbohydrate intake before selected low-intensity sessions may enhance mitochondrial function and fat oxidative capacity, but may compromise performance during high-intensity efforts.

Protein intakes of approximately 1.6 g/kg/day are necessary to maintain lean mass and support recovery, with up to 2.5 g/kg/day warranted during demanding training periods with greater calorie requirements.
During ultra-marathon racing, runners should aim to consume 150-400 kcal/h (carbohydrate 30-50 g/h, protein 5-10 g/h) from a variety of calorie-dense foods to attenuate caloric deficits.
Fluid intake of 450-750 mL/h (approximately 150-250 mL every 20 minutes) is recommended during ultra-marathon racing, with sodium concentrations greater than 575 mg/L to minimize hyponatraemia risk.
Progressive gut-training and/or low-FODMAP diets can alleviate symptoms of gastrointestinal distress during ultra-marathon racing.
Evidence does not support ketogenic diets and/or ketone esters for improving ultra-marathon performance.
Strategic use of caffeine can sustain performance in the latter stages of ultra-marathon racing, particularly when sleep deprivation may compromise athlete safety.
